lem:localized-clipped-drift-bound.proof_tex: Fix the training folds and write Delta_a=muhat_a-mu_a and b_q(x)=E_P[Gamma_q(O;etahat)|X=x]-tau_P(x). Lem:clip-bias gives b_q=(ehat_q-e_P){Delta_1/ehat_q+Delta_0/(1-ehat_q)}. Since the clipped denominators are at least q, |b_q|<=q^{-1}|ehat_q-e_P|(|Delta_1|+|Delta_0|). Let I_q=[q,1-q] and Pi_q be projection onto I_q. Then ehat_q=Pi_q(ehat) and |ehat_q-e_P|<=|ehat-e_P|+dist(e_P,I_q)<=|ehat-e_P|+q 1{p_P<=q}. For any policy pi with regret r, the deterministic drift contribution is bounded by E[1_{D_pi}|b_q|]. The first part is at most q^{-1} sum_a E[|ehat-e_P||Delta_a|]<=C r_mu_n r_e_n/q by Cauchy-Schwarz and ass:nuisance-rate. For gamma>0, the second part is at most sum_a E[1_{D_pi}1{p_P<=q}|Delta_a|]<=C r_mu_n P_X(D_pi intersect {p_P<=q})^{1/2}. Applying lem:clipped-region-localization and sqrt{x+y}<=sqrt{x}+sqrt{y} gives C r_mu_n{u^{alpha/2}q^{1/(2 gamma)}+(r/u)^{1/2}} whenever q<=c_o u^gamma. This proves the gamma>0 bound. When gamma=0 and q<=underline_p/2, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint implies e_P in I_q almost surely, so dist(e_P,I_q)=0 and |ehat_q-e_P|<=|ehat-e_P|. The same Cauchy-Schwarz step gives E[1_{D_pi}|b_q|]<=C r_mu_n r_e_n, with the fixed q absorbed into C.

snapshot.stmt: Let b_q(x)=E_P[Gamma_q(O;etahat)|X=x]-tau_P(x), with cross-fitted nuisances fixed on the evaluation fold. For gamma>0, any pi with r=R_P(pi) satisfies |P[(pi-pi_star)b_q]| <= C{r_mu_n r_e_n/q + r_mu_n u^{alpha/2}q^{1/(2 gamma)} + r_mu_n(r/u)^{1/2}} whenever q<=c_o u^gamma. For gamma=0 and fixed q<=underline_p/2, |P[(pi-pi_star)b_q]| <= C r_mu_n r_e_n.

defs.def:clipped-propensity: e_q(x; eta) = min{1-q, max{q, e(x)}} for clip q in (0,1/2] and nuisance eta=(mu_0,mu_1,e).

assumptions.ass:nuisance-rate: The cross-fitted nuisance estimators satisfy ||muhat_a-mu_a||_{L2(P)} <= r_mu_n and ||ehat-e||_{L2(P)} <= r_e_n for a in {0,1}, with product-rate r_mu_n r_e_n = O(n^{-1/2}).
assumptions.ass:strict-overlap-endpoint: When gamma=0, p_P(X) >= underline_p holds P_X-almost surely, for a constant underline_p in (0,1/2].

lem:crude-clipped-score-envelope.proof_tex: By def:clipped-propensity, the clipped denominator e_q lies in [q,1-q], so both e_q and 1-e_q are at least q. By ass:bounded-outcome and ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances, |Y-muhat_a(X)|<=2 and |muhat_1(X)-muhat_0(X)|<=2. In def:clipped-aipw-score only one of the inverse-propensity residual terms is active because A is binary. Therefore |Gamma_q(O;etahat)|<=2+2/q<=C/q for q<=1/2. Squaring gives the conditional second-moment bound.

defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).

lem:clipped-region-localization.proof_tex:
Proof. Let D=D_pi. By ass:zero-effect, the portion of D on {tau_P=0} has P_X-measure zero. Hence, up to a null set,
D intersect {p_P<=q} subset {p_P<=q, 0<|tau_P|<=u} union (D intersect {|tau_P|>u}).
The first set has probability at most C_o u^alpha q^{1/gamma} by ass:overlap-decay because q<=c_o u^gamma. For the second set, thm:welfare-identity gives r=int_D |tau_P| dP_X, so P_X(D intersect {|tau_P|>u})<=r/u. Adding the two bounds proves the claim.

snapshot.stmt: For gamma>0, under ass:overlap-decay and ass:zero-effect, every policy pi with regret r=R_P(pi) satisfies P_X(D_pi intersect {p_P<=q}) <= C u^alpha q^{1/gamma}+r/u whenever 0<u<=u_0 and q<=c_o u^gamma.

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:zero-effect: Either P_X{tau_P(X)=0}=0, or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star P_X-almost everywhere on {tau_P(X)=0}.

node.statement: For gamma>0, under ass:overlap-decay and ass:zero-effect, every policy pi with regret r=R_P(pi) satisfies P_X(D_pi intersect {p_P<=q}) <= C u^alpha q^{1/gamma}+r/u whenever 0<u<=u_0 and q<=c_o u^gamma.

Proof. Let D=D_pi. By ass:zero-effect, the portion of D on {tau_P=0} has P_X-measure zero. Hence, up to a null set,
D intersect {p_P<=q} subset {p_P<=q, 0<|tau_P|<=u} union (D intersect {|tau_P|>u}).
The first set has probability at most C_o u^alpha q^{1/gamma} by ass:overlap-decay because q<=c_o u^gamma. For the second set, thm:welfare-identity gives r=int_D |tau_P| dP_X, so P_X(D intersect {|tau_P|>u})<=r/u. Adding the two bounds proves the claim.

thm:welfare-identity.proof_tex:
Let \(\pi\in\Pi\) be deterministic. By the definition of welfare regret,
\[
R_P(\pi)=V_P(\pi_\star)-V_P(\pi)
=E_P\{(\pi_\star(X)-\pi(X))\tau_P(X)\}.
\]
The bounded-outcome assumption gives \(\mu_a(X)\in[-1,1]\), hence \(\tau_P\in[-2,2]\), so the expectation is finite. Pointwise in \(x\), if \(\tau_P(x)>0\) then \(\pi_\star(x)=1\) and
\[(\pi_\star(x)-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=\tau_P(x){\bf 1}\{\pi(x)=0\}=|\tau_P(x)|{\bf 1}\{\pi(x)\ne\pi_\star(x)\}.
\]
If \(\tau_P(x)<0\), then \(\pi_\star(x)=0\) and
\[(\pi_\star(x)-\pi(x))\tau_P(x)=-\pi(x)\tau_P(x)=|\tau_P(x)|{\bf 1}\{\pi(x)\ne\pi_\star(x)\}.
\]
If \(\tau_P(x)=0\), both sides are zero, independently of the tie convention in \(\pi_\star\). Therefore
\[
(\pi_\star(X)-\pi(X))\tau_P(X)=|\tau_P(X)|{\bf 1}\{\pi(X)\ne\pi_\star(X)\}
\quad P_X\text{-a.s.}
\]
Taking expectation and then writing the expectation under the covariate marginal gives
\[
R_P(\pi)=E_P[|\tau_P(X)|{\bf 1}\{\pi(X)\ne\pi_\star(X)\}]
=\int_{\mathcal X}|\tau_P(x)|{\bf 1}\{\pi(x)\ne\pi_\star(x)\}\,dP_X(x).
\]
The iid sampling assumption is part of the statistical setup but is not needed for this population identity.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:iid and ass:bounded-outcome, for every deterministic pi in Pi, R_P(pi) = E_P[ |tau_P(X)| 1{pi(X) != pi_star(X)} ] = integral over X of |tau_P(x)| 1{pi(x) != pi_star(x)} dP_X(x).

defs.def:welfare-regret: V_P(pi) = E_P[pi(X) tau_P(X)]; pi_star(x) = 1{tau_P(x) >= 0}; R_P(pi) = V_P(pi_star) - V_P(pi).

thm:margin-localization.proof_tex:
Proof. Fix a deterministic policy pi in Pi and write D=D_pi. By thm:welfare-identity, R_P(pi)=int_D |tau_P(x)| dP_X(x). The integrand is nonnegative and finite by ass:bounded-outcome.

First consider the corner case R_P(pi)=0. Then |tau_P| 1_D=0 P_X-almost surely. Hence P_X(D intersect {0<|tau_P|})=0. On the remaining zero-effect set, ass:zero-effect says either P_X{tau_P=0}=0 or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star there P_X-almost everywhere. In both alternatives P_X(D intersect {tau_P=0})=0. Therefore P_X(D)=0, so the desired inequality holds with the convention 0^{alpha/(1+alpha)}=0.

Now assume r:=R_P(pi)>0. For any u in (0,u_0], decompose, up to the P_X-null zero-effect disagreement set,
D subset {0<|tau_P|<=u} union (D intersect {|tau_P|>u}).
By ass:margin, P_X(0<|tau_P(X)|<=u)<=C_m u^alpha. By thm:welfare-identity, r>=u P_X(D intersect {|tau_P|>u}), so P_X(D intersect {|tau_P|>u})<=r/u. Thus
P_X(D)<=C_m u^alpha+r/u, 0<u<=u_0.
If r^{1/(1+alpha)}<=u_0, take u=r^{1/(1+alpha)} and obtain P_X(D)<=(C_m+1) r^{alpha/(1+alpha)}. If r^{1/(1+alpha)}>u_0, then P_X(D)<=1<=u_0^{-alpha} r^{alpha/(1+alpha)} when alpha>0, and for alpha=0 simply P_X(D)<=1. Enlarging the constant gives a single C=C(C_m,u_0,alpha) such that P_X(D_pi)<=C R_P(pi)^{alpha/(1+alpha)} for all pi.

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:margin and ass:zero-effect there is C=C(C_m,u_0,alpha) such that for every pi in Pi, P_X(D_pi) <= C R_P(pi)^(alpha/(1+alpha)).

assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:zero-effect: Either P_X{tau_P(X)=0}=0, or every pi in Pi agrees with pi_star P_X-almost everywhere on {tau_P(X)=0}.

prop:overlap-envelope.proof_tex:
Assume \(\gamma>0\). For \(v=h^\beta\) and \(u=h^{\beta/\gamma}\), direct algebra gives
\[
u^\alpha v^{1/\gamma}
=(h^{\beta/\gamma})^\alpha (h^\beta)^{1/\gamma}
=h^{(\alpha+1)\beta/\gamma}.
\]
Since \(0<h<1\), \(h^a\ge h^b\) if and only if \(a\le b\). Hence
\[
u^\alpha v^{1/\gamma}\ge h^\alpha
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\frac{(\alpha+1)\beta}{\gamma}\le \alpha
\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad
\beta\le \frac{\alpha\gamma}{\alpha+1}.
\]
By def:exponents, the right endpoint is \(\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\), so equality holds at \(\beta=\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\).

Now consider a block whose \(P_X\)-mass is a constant multiple of \(h^\alpha\), whose contrast height is of order \(h\), and whose weak-arm propensity is of order \(h^\beta\). The overlap-decay condition is most restrictive at the first \(u\)-window for which the constraint \(v\le c_o u^\gamma\) permits \(v\asymp h^\beta\), namely \(u\asymp h^{\beta/\gamma}\). At this window the right side of ass:overlap-decay is, up to constants, \(h^{(\alpha+1)\beta/\gamma}\). The block mass \(h^\alpha\) is therefore admissible up to constants exactly when \(\beta\le\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\). If \(\beta>\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\), the allowed envelope is \(o(h^\alpha)\), so no fixed positive multiple of \(h^\alpha\) can satisfy the inequality for all small \(h\). Thus \(\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\) is the largest admissible weak-arm exponent, equivalently the least informative admissible weak-arm scale.

snapshot.stmt: For alpha >= 0, gamma > 0, h in (0,1), beta >= 0, at the tight window v=h^beta, u=h^(beta/gamma): u^alpha v^(1/gamma) = h^((alpha+1)beta/gamma), and u^alpha v^(1/gamma) >= h^alpha iff beta <= alpha gamma/(alpha+1), with equality at beta = beta_ag. Hence a block of mass ~ h^alpha, contrast ~ h, propensity ~ h^beta meets the ass:overlap-decay envelope iff beta <= beta_ag, and beta_ag is the least informative admissible weak-arm exponent.

defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.

lem:witness-membership.proof_tex:
Proof. Write h=h_n, beta=beta_ag, m=P_X(B_n)=c_B h^alpha, and let sigma be either + or -. The construction has P_X equal to Lebesgue measure on [0,1], propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n, and propensity e(x)=1/2 on B_n^c. For all large n, h is in (0,1). If beta>0 then q_n=h^beta is in (0,1/2]; if beta=0 the corrected construction uses the fixed value q_n=1/4. Hence 0<e(x)<1 everywhere, proving ass:positivity. The sampling condition ass:iid is satisfied by taking O_1,...,O_n to be independent draws from the constructed law.

Define potential outcomes conditionally on X and then draw A with propensity e(X) independently of (Y(0),Y(1)) given X. On B_n set Y(0)=0 and take Y(1) in {-1,1} with P(Y(1)=1 | X=x)=(1+sigma h)/2; then E[Y(1)-Y(0) | X=x]=sigma h. On B_n^c choose bounded conditional laws on [-1,1] whose mean contrast is tau_0 in (u_0,2). Such a tau_0 exists by ass:margin-window, and bounded means can be realized for instance with means -tau_0/2 and tau_0/2. Finally set Y=A Y(1)+(1-A)Y(0). This gives ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, and ass:bounded-outcome, and realizes the observed conditional means in def:two-point-witness.

The contrast is tau_{P_{n,+}}(x)=h on B_n, tau_{P_{n,-}}(x)=-h on B_n, and tau_{P_{n,sigma}}(x)=tau_0>u_0 on B_n^c. Thus tau is never zero, so ass:zero-effect holds. The pointwise sign gives the two optimal policies: under P_{n,+}, pi_star(x)=1 for all x; under P_{n,-}, pi_star(x)=0 on B_n and 1 on B_n^c, namely x -> 1{x notin B_n}.

We verify ass:margin. For 0<u<=u_0, the event {0<|tau(X)|<=u} is empty if u<h, and is B_n if h<=u<=u_0, since the off-block contrast is tau_0>u_0. Hence P(0<|tau(X)|<=u) <= c_B h^alpha 1{u>=h} <= c_B u^alpha. Choose the free block constant c_B<=C_m, compatibly with the small-constant information requirement in def:two-point-witness. Then ass:margin holds.

We verify ass:overlap-decay. For 0<u<=u_0 and 0<v<=c_o u^gamma, the event {p_P(X)<=v, 0<|tau(X)|<=u} is empty unless u>=h and v>=q_n; when it is nonempty it is contained in B_n and has mass c_B h^alpha. If gamma=0, the convention is v^(1/gamma)=1, and the desired inequality follows from c_B h^alpha <= C_o u^alpha whenever u>=h after choosing c_B<=C_o. Now suppose gamma>0. If beta>0, the constraints v>=h^beta and v<=c_o u^gamma imply u>=(h^beta/c_o)^(1/gamma), and therefore u^alpha v^(1/gamma) >= (h^beta/c_o)^(alpha/gamma) h^(beta/gamma) = c_o^(-alpha/gamma) h^((alpha+1)beta/gamma). By def:exponents and prop:overlap-envelope, ((alpha+1)beta/gamma)=alpha. Thus the right side of ass:overlap-decay is at least C_o c_o^(-alpha/gamma) h^alpha, and the bound follows after choosing c_B<=C_o c_o^(-alpha/gamma). If gamma>0 and beta=0, then alpha=0; the block mass is c_B, q_n=1/4, and nonemptiness implies v>=1/4, so C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma)=C_o v^(1/gamma)>=C_o 4^(-1/gamma). Choose c_B<=C_o 4^(-1/gamma). These finitely many smallness requirements can all be imposed once by decreasing c_B.

Finally, ass:strict-overlap-endpoint is active only when gamma=0. In that case q_n=1/4 on B_n and e=1/2 off B_n, so p_P(X)>=1/4 almost surely; the endpoint condition holds with underline_p=1/4 or any smaller fixed constant. Therefore P_{n,+} and P_{n,-} satisfy every member property listed in def:law-class for all large n, so both laws lie in P_class(alpha,gamma). The two witness-optimal policies were identified above, and if they belong to Pi they are available as the two policy actions used in the two-point lower-bound reduction.

snapshot.stmt: For all large n the corrected witness laws P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} satisfy ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, and ass:strict-overlap-endpoint, hence P_{n,+}, P_{n,-} lie in def:law-class P_class(alpha,gamma). The two explicit witness-optimal policies are x -> 1 and x -> 1{x notin B_n}; if these policies belong to Pi, they are available as the two policy actions used by the later two-point lower-bound reduction. (Normalization: the margin window satisfies u_0<2, so an off-block contrast tau_0 in (u_0, 2) exists under bounded outcomes |Y|<=1.)

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.
defs.def:law-class: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, and ass:strict-overlap-endpoint hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }

assumptions.ass:overlap-decay: P{p_P(X) <= v, 0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u} <= C_o u^alpha v^(1/gamma) for all 0 < v <= c_o u^gamma, with v^(1/gamma)=1 when gamma=0.
assumptions.ass:margin: P(0 < |tau_P(X)| <= u) <= C_m u^alpha for all 0 < u <= u_0.
assumptions.ass:iid: O_1, ..., O_n are i.i.d. draws from P.
assumptions.ass:unconfoundedness: (Y(0), Y(1)) is independent of A given X under P.
assumptions.ass:strict-overlap-endpoint: When gamma=0, p_P(X) >= underline_p holds P_X-almost surely, for a constant underline_p in (0,1/2].

lem:two-point-divergence.proof_tex:
Let \(m_n=P_X(B_n)=c_Bh_n^\alpha\). The two laws differ only on the cell \(\{X\in B_n,A=1\}\), whose probability under either law is \(m_nq_n\). Conditional on that cell, the outcome is supported on \(\{-1,1\}\) with probabilities \((1+h_n)/2,(1-h_n)/2\) under \(P_{n,+}\) and the probabilities reversed under \(P_{n,-}\). For \(h_n\le1/2\), the Bernoulli chi-square divergence is
\[
\chi^2\!\left(\frac{1+h_n}{2},\frac{1-h_n}{2}\right)
=\frac{h_n^2}{(1-h_n)/2}+\frac{h_n^2}{(1+h_n)/2}
=\frac{4h_n^2}{1-h_n^2}
\le 8h_n^2.
\]
Since the mixing probabilities and all other conditional laws are identical, the per-observation divergence is the cell mass times this conditional divergence:
\[
\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-})\le 8m_nq_nh_n^2
\le C h_n^{2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}},
\]
with the usual interpretation \(q_n\asymp h_n^{\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}}\), including the constant weak-arm scale when \(\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}=0\). By def:exponents, \(D_{\alpha,\gamma}=2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,\gamma}\) and \(h_n=n^{-1/D_{\alpha,\gamma}}\), so the right side is at most \(C/n\).

Chi-square tensorizes as
\[
1+\chi^2(P_{n,+}^{\otimes n}\|P_{n,-}^{\otimes n})
=(1+\chi^2(P_{n,+}\|P_{n,-}))^n.
\]
Therefore
\[
1+\chi^2(P_{n,+}^{\otimes n}\|P_{n,-}^{\otimes n})
\le (1+C/n)^n\le e^C,
\]
and the \(n\)-fold chi-square divergence is bounded by a constant independent of \(n\).

snapshot.stmt: For the def:two-point-witness laws, the per-observation chi-square is chi^2(P_{n,+} || P_{n,-}) <= C m_n q_n h_n^2 with m_n=P_X(B_n)~h_n^alpha and q_n~h_n^{beta_ag}, hence ~ h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}; for h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)} the n-fold divergence is bounded, chi^2(P_{n,+}^{⊗n} || P_{n,-}^{⊗n}) <= C (equivalently 1+chi^2(P^{⊗n}) = (1+O(h_n^{2+alpha+beta_ag}))^n <= e^{C}).

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

lem:regret-separation.proof_tex:
Proof. Write h=h_n and m=P_X(B_n). By def:two-point-witness, on B_n the contrasts are tau_{P_{n,+}}=h and tau_{P_{n,-}}=-h. Therefore the two optimal labels on B_n are opposite: pi_star,+ equals 1 and pi_star,- equals 0 on B_n. For any deterministic binary policy pi and every x in B_n, exactly one of the two indicators 1{pi(x) != pi_star,+(x)} and 1{pi(x) != pi_star,-(x)} is equal to one.

Applying thm:welfare-identity under each witness law, and using ass:bounded-outcome to justify that identity, gives
R_{P_{n,+}}(pi)+R_{P_{n,-}}(pi) >= h int_{B_n} [1{pi(x) != pi_star,+(x)}+1{pi(x) != pi_star,-(x)}] dP_X(x) = h P_X(B_n)=h m.
Hence max_{sigma in {+,-}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= h m/2. The construction has m=c_B h^alpha, so max_sigma R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= (c_B/2) h^{1+alpha}. Taking the infimum over pi in Pi proves the claim with c=c_B/2.

snapshot.stmt: For the def:two-point-witness laws, inf_{pi in Pi} max_{sigma in {+,-}} R_{P_{n,sigma}}(pi) >= c h_n^(1+alpha): the optimal policies are opposite on B_n, so any pi misclassifies B_n under at least one law and pays regret >= (1/2) P_X(B_n) h_n ~ h_n^(1+alpha).

defs.def:two-point-witness: On X=[0,1] with P_X=Lebesgue, fix h_n=n^{-1/(2+alpha+beta_ag)}, q_n=(1/4 if beta_ag=0 else h_n^{beta_ag}), and an active block B_n=[0, c_B h_n^alpha] with P_X(B_n)=c_B h_n^alpha. The two laws P_{n,sigma}, sigma in {+,-}, share P_X, share the logging propensity e(x)=q_n on B_n and e(x)=1/2 off B_n, and share an off-block outcome law with common optimal label 1 and contrast tau_0 > u_0. On B_n set mu_0(x)=0 and let the treated outcome on the active treated cell {X in B_n, A=1} satisfy P(Y=1 | X in B_n, A=1)=(1+sigma h_n)/2, so tau_{P_{n,sigma}}=sigma h_n on B_n. The laws agree everywhere off the treated active cell. Constants are chosen with 8 C_B C_Q < log 5.

lem:le-cam-two-point-chisq.proof_tex:
Let \(P=P_+^{\otimes n}\), \(Q=P_-^{\otimes n}\), and let \(\psi\) be any test taking values in \(\{+,-\}\). The sum of testing errors is
\[
  P(\psi\ne +)+Q(\psi\ne -)=P(\psi=-)+Q(\psi=+).
\]
The minimal value over all tests equals \(1-\|P-Q\|_{TV}\): this follows by writing the risk of the rejection region \(A=\{\psi=+\}\) as \(P(A^c)+Q(A)=1-P(A)+Q(A)\), and maximizing \(P(A)-Q(A)\) over measurable \(A\), whose supremum is total variation.

We now bound total variation by chi-square in a form that is positive for every finite chi-square bound. Assume \(P\ll Q\), write \(L=dP/dQ\), and set \(A=\{L\ge1\}\). Then \(t:=\|P-Q\|_{TV}=P(A)-Q(A)=\int_A(L-1)dQ\). By Cauchy-Schwarz,
\[
  t^2\le Q(A)\int_A(L-1)^2dQ\le Q(A)\chi^2(P\|Q).
\]
Since \(P(A)=Q(A)+t\le1\), we have \(Q(A)\le1-t\). If \(\chi^2(P\|Q)\le C_\chi\), then
\[
  t^2\le C_\chi(1-t).
\]
Solving the quadratic inequality gives
\[
  t\le \rho(C_\chi):={\sqrt{C_\chi^2+4C_\chi}-C_\chi\over2}<1.
\]
Therefore every test satisfies
\[
  P_+^n(\psi\ne +)+P_-^n(\psi\ne -)
  \ge 1-\rho(C_\chi)=:c(C_\chi)>0.
\]

For the per-observation implication, if \(\chi^2(P_+\|P_-)\le c_0/n\), then the product identity gives
\[
  1+
  \chi^2(P_+^{\otimes n}\|P_-^{\otimes n})
  =\{1+
  \chi^2(P_+\|P_-)\}^n
  \le (1+c_0/n)^n\le e^{c_0}.
\]
Thus \(\chi^2(P_+^{\otimes n}\|P_-^{\otimes n})\le e^{c_0}-1\), and the preceding bound applies with \(C_\chi=e^{c_0}-1\).

snapshot.stmt: In-core two-point testing lemma. If chi^2(P_+^{\otimes n} || P_-^{\otimes n}) <= C_chi, then every test psi has P_+^n(psi != +)+P_-^n(psi != -) >= c(C_chi)>0; in particular the conclusion follows from a per-observation chi-square bound <= c_0/n using the product chi-square identity. Prove this from total-variation testing risk, Cauchy-Schwarz/Pinsker-type TV <= sqrt(chi^2), and (1+x/n)^n <= exp(x), not by citation alone.

thm:minimax-lower.proof_tex:
Proof. Fix n large enough that lem:witness-membership and lem:two-point-divergence apply, and write P_+=P_{n,+} and P_-=P_{n,-}. By lem:witness-membership, both alternatives belong to P_class(alpha,gamma). Therefore, for any measurable Pi-valued estimator pi_hat, the risk sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat) is at least max{E_{P_+} R_{P_+}(pi_hat), E_{P_-} R_{P_-}(pi_hat)}.

Let Delta_n=c_sep h_n^{1+alpha} be the separation from lem:regret-separation. For each realized data set, define a test psi by psi=+ if R_{P_+}(pi_hat)<=R_{P_-}(pi_hat), and psi=- otherwise, with arbitrary tie-breaking on equality. On the event {psi=-}, if R_{P_+}(pi_hat)<Delta_n/2, then the definition of psi gives R_{P_-}(pi_hat)<Delta_n/2 as well, contradicting lem:regret-separation. Hence R_{P_+}(pi_hat)>=Delta_n/2 on {psi=-}. Similarly, R_{P_-}(pi_hat)>=Delta_n/2 on {psi=+}. Consequently
E_{P_+} R_{P_+}(pi_hat)+E_{P_-} R_{P_-}(pi_hat) >= (Delta_n/2) {P_+^n(psi=-)+P_-^n(psi=+)}.

By lem:two-point-divergence, chi^2(P_+^{otimes n} || P_-^{otimes n}) is bounded by a constant C_chi independent of n. Lem:le-cam-two-point-chisq gives c_test=c(C_chi)>0 such that every test satisfies P_+^n(psi=-)+P_-^n(psi=+)>=c_test. Hence max_sigma E_{P_sigma} R_{P_sigma}(pi_hat) >= c_test Delta_n/4. Since pi_hat was arbitrary, taking the infimum over all measurable Pi-valued estimators in def:minimax-regret yields M_n(alpha,gamma)>=c h_n^{1+alpha}.

Finally, def:exponents gives h_n=n^{-1/D_ag} and r_star(alpha,gamma)=(1+alpha)/D_ag. Thus h_n^{1+alpha}=n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)}, proving M_n(alpha,gamma)>=c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} for all large n.

snapshot.stmt: M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf_{pi_hat} sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat) >= c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} for all large n, with c > 0 independent of n.

defs.def:minimax-regret: M_n(alpha,gamma) = inf over measurable data-dependent Pi-valued estimators pi_hat of sup_{P in P_class} E_P R_P(pi_hat).
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

thm:rate-characterization.proof_tex: Proof. The theorem is the lower-bound-only headline. By thm:minimax-lower, there exist constants c>0 and N<infinity such that for all n>=N, M_n(alpha,gamma)>=c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)}. By def:exponents, D_ag=2+alpha+beta_ag and r_star(alpha,gamma)=(1+alpha)/D_ag=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_{alpha,gamma}). This is exactly the displayed sharp lower-bound characterization. No feasible-upper theorem and no tightness residual is used in this corollary.

snapshot.stmt: Sharp lower-bound characterization. M_n(alpha,gamma) >= c n^{-r_star(alpha,gamma)} for all large n, where r_star=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha+beta_{alpha,gamma}).

defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

lem:clip-bias.proof_tex:
Fix \(x\) and write \(e=e_P(x)\), \(\bar e_q=\bar e_q(x)\), \(\mu_a=\mu_a(x)\), \(\bar\mu_a=\bar\mu_a(x)\), and \(\Delta_a=\bar\mu_a-\mu_a\). By def:clipped-aipw-score and the defining conditional means,
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]
=\bar\mu_1-\bar\mu_0+\frac{e}{\bar e_q}(\mu_1-\bar\mu_1)-\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}(\mu_0-\bar\mu_0).
\]
Substitute \(\bar\mu_a=\mu_a+\Delta_a\):
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]
=\mu_1-\mu_0+\Delta_1-\Delta_0-\frac{e}{\bar e_q}\Delta_1+\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}\Delta_0.
\]
Since \(\tau_P(x)=\mu_1-\mu_0\), the drift equals
\[
\Delta_1\left(1-\frac{e}{\bar e_q}\right)
+\Delta_0\left(-1+\frac{1-e}{1-\bar e_q}\right)
=(\bar e_q-e)\left(\frac{\Delta_1}{\bar e_q}+\frac{\Delta_0}{1-\bar e_q}\right),
\]
which is the displayed identity. Positivity and clipping ensure the denominators are nonzero. The identity shows that double-robust cancellation occurs when \(\bar e_q=e_P\) or \(\Delta_0=\Delta_1=0\); the event \(p_P>q\) alone does not imply \(\bar e_q=e_P\) for an estimated propensity, so no additional clipped-region cancellation follows from the frozen assumptions.

snapshot.stmt:
Exact clipped-score drift identity. For any measurable plug-in nuisance triple \(\bar\eta=(\bar\mu_0,\bar\mu_1,\bar e)\), with \(\bar e_q=\min\{1-q,\max\{q,\bar e\}\}\) and \(\Delta_a=\bar\mu_a-\mu_a\), the conditional mean drift satisfies
\[
E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\bar\eta)\mid X=x]-\tau_P(x)
=(\bar e_q(x)-e_P(x))\left(\frac{\Delta_1(x)}{\bar e_q(x)}+\frac{\Delta_0(x)}{1-\bar e_q(x)}\right).
\]
Hence cancellation holds only where \(\bar e_q(x)=e_P(x)\) or both regression errors vanish; it does not follow merely from \(p_P(x)>q\). Under the frozen assumptions alone, no additive clipped-region bound of the form asserted in the current lemma is derivable.

defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).

lem:feasible-erm-basic-inequality.proof_tex:
Proof. Let \(\Pi_0=(\pi_j)_{j\ge1}\) be the countable pointwise-dense subclass supplied by ass:policy-class. For a realized sample and fixed cross-fitted nuisance scores \(\Gamma_i=\Gamma_q(O_i;\widehat\eta^{(-k(i))})\), the empirical criterion is
\[
\widehat V_{n,q}(\pi)=n^{-1}\sum_{i=1}^n \pi(X_i)\Gamma_i .
\]
If \(\pi\in\Pi\), pointwise density gives \(\pi_{j_\ell}(x)\to\pi(x)\) for every \(x\). Since \(\widehat V_{n,q}\) is a finite sum over the observed covariates, \(\widehat V_{n,q}(\pi_{j_\ell})\to\widehat V_{n,q}(\pi)\). Hence
\[
\sup_{\pi\in\Pi}\widehat V_{n,q}(\pi)=\sup_{j\ge1}\widehat V_{n,q}(\pi_j).
\]
The right side is a countable supremum of measurable sample functions. The index \(j_n\) in def:feasible-erm is the first integer satisfying \(\widehat V_{n,q}(\pi_j)\ge \sup_k \widehat V_{n,q}(\pi_k)-1/n\); this event is measurable because it is determined by countably many measurable inequalities. Therefore \(\widehat\pi_n=\pi_{j_n}\) is a measurable \(\Pi\)-valued estimator and, by construction,
\[
\widehat V_{n,q}(\widehat\pi_n)\ge \sup_{\pi\in\Pi}\widehat V_{n,q}(\pi)-1/n.
\]
For any comparator \(\pi^b\in\Pi\), subtracting \(\widehat V_{n,q}(\pi^b)\) gives
\[
P_n[(\widehat\pi_n-\pi^b)\Gamma_q]\ge -1/n.
\]
Under ass:optimal-in-class, \(\pi_\star\in\Pi\), so the comparator inequality applies to \(\pi^b=\pi_\star\).

snapshot.stmt: Under ass:policy-class, def:feasible-erm defines a measurable Pi-valued estimator satisfying Vhat_{n,q}(pi_hat_n) >= sup_{pi in Pi} Vhat_{n,q}(pi) - 1/n. Hence for every comparator pi^b in Pi, P_n[(pi_hat_n-pi^b) Gamma_q] >= -1/n; under ass:optimal-in-class this applies to pi^b=pi_star.

defs.def:feasible-erm: Fix a countable pointwise-dense subclass Pi_0=(pi_j)_{j>=1} from ass:policy-class and a deterministic balanced K-fold partition I_1,...,I_K of {1,...,n}, where each fold has size floor(n/K) or ceil(n/K). With foldwise cross-fitted nuisances etahat^{(-k)} and clip q in (0,1/2], define Vhat_{n,q}(pi)=n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}), where k(i) is the evaluation fold of observation i. Let j_n be the smallest index j such that Vhat_{n,q}(pi_j) >= sup_k Vhat_{n,q}(pi_k) - 1/n, and set pi_hat_n=pi_{j_n}. This measurable Pi-valued 1/n-ERM is the feasible clipped-AIPW ERM. The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter chosen by the bias-variance balance.

assumptions.ass:policy-class: Pi is a pointwise measurable class of measurable deterministic policies with finite VC dimension d_Pi < infinity: there is a countable subclass Pi_0 subset Pi such that every pi in Pi is the pointwise limit of a sequence from Pi_0.
assumptions.ass:optimal-in-class: pi_star is an element of Pi for every law P in the class.

lem:localized-vc-process-bound.proof_tex:
Proof. Fix a regret radius \(r\ge0\) and set \(\Pi(r)=\{\pi\in\Pi:R_P(\pi)\le r\}\). For every \(\pi\in\Pi(r)\), thm:margin-localization gives
\[
P_X(D_\pi)\le C R_P(\pi)^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)}\le C r^{\alpha/(1+\alpha)} .
\]
The increments \(g_\pi\) are centered, have envelope \(B\), and satisfy the conditional second-moment bound \(E[g_\pi^2]\le C B^2P_X(D_\pi)\) by the hypothesis of the lemma. The atomic fixed-radius empirical-process assumption ass:vc-localized-envelope is exactly the finite-VC localized bound for such pointwise measurable policy-indexed classes after applying the preceding margin localization. With sample size \(m\), it yields
\[
E_P\sup_{\pi\in\Pi(r)} |(P_m-P)g_\pi|
\le C B m^{-1/2} r^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}(\log m)^p .
\]
Since \(\kappa=\alpha/(2+2\alpha)\), this is the claimed bound for \(Z_m(r)\). The cases \(r=0\) and \(\alpha=0\) are included by the conventions in thm:margin-localization and by enlarging the universal constant.

snapshot.stmt: Let kappa=alpha/(2+2 alpha). For an i.i.d. sample of size m, let Z_m(r)=sup_{pi in Pi:R_P(pi)<=r} |(P_m-P) g_pi|, where the centered increments g_pi have envelope B and conditional second moment bounded by C B^2 P_X(D_pi). Under ass:vc-localized-envelope and thm:margin-localization, E_P Z_m(r) <= C B m^(-1/2) r^kappa (log m)^p for every regret radius r.

assumptions.ass:vc-localized-envelope: For the pointwise measurable finite-VC policy class Pi, every centered policy-indexed process with envelope B and conditional second moment bounded by C B^2 P_X(D_pi) has fixed-radius localized supremum bounded by C B n^{-1/2} r^{alpha/(2+2 alpha)}(log n)^p under thm:margin-localization.

lem:crossfit-localized-process-reduction.proof_tex:
Proof. Let the deterministic folds be \(I_1,\ldots,I_K\), write \(n_k=|I_k|\), and set \(w_k=n_k/n\). For the foldwise fixed nuisance object define
\[
Z_k(r)=\sup_{\pi:R_P(\pi)\le r}\left|(P_{I_k}-P)g_{k,\pi}\right|,
\]
where \(g_{k,\pi}\) denotes the centered evaluation-fold increment. The pooled process has the form
\[
Z_{cf}(r)=\sup_{\pi:R_P(\pi)\le r}\left|\sum_{k=1}^K w_k(P_{I_k}-P)g_{k,\pi}\right| .
\]
By the triangle inequality,
\[
Z_{cf}(r)\le \sum_{k=1}^K w_k Z_k(r).
\]
For a fixed fold \(k\), condition on the training sigma-field used to build \(g_{k,\pi}\). By ass:iid and the deterministic fold partition in def:feasible-erm, the observations in \(I_k\) are then i.i.d. from \(P\), and the foldwise class is fixed. The assumed envelope and second-moment bounds allow lem:localized-vc-process-bound to be applied conditionally, giving
\[
E_P[Z_k(r)\mid \mathcal T_k]
\le C B n_k^{-1/2} r^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}(\log n_k)^p .
\]
Taking expectations and summing with weights gives
\[
E_P Z_{cf}(r)
\le C B r^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}\sum_{k=1}^K w_kn_k^{-1/2}(\log n_k)^p .
\]
By ass:fixed-crossfit-fold-count, \(K\) is fixed and the folds are balanced, so \(n_k\asymp n\), \(\log n_k\le C\log n\), and \(\sum_kw_kn_k^{-1/2}\le C n^{-1/2}\). Therefore
\[
E_P Z_{cf}(r)
\le C B n^{-1/2} r^{\alpha/(2+2\alpha)}(\log n)^p .
\]
The displayed conditional estimate is the foldwise conditional estimate used in cross-fitting; integrating over the training folds gives the unconditional bound claimed for the pooled process.

snapshot.stmt: Let Z_cf(r) be the pooled cross-fit localized process obtained by averaging centered evaluation-fold increments. If ass:fixed-crossfit-fold-count holds, then conditional on the training folds each balanced evaluation fold is i.i.d.; if the centered foldwise increments have envelope B and conditional second moment bounded by C B^2 P_X(D_pi), then under ass:vc-localized-envelope and thm:margin-localization, E_P[Z_cf(r) | training folds] <= C B n^(-1/2) r^(alpha/(2+2 alpha)) (log n)^p, hence the same bound unconditionally.

defs.def:feasible-erm: Fix a countable pointwise-dense subclass Pi_0=(pi_j)_{j>=1} from ass:policy-class and a deterministic balanced K-fold partition I_1,...,I_K of {1,...,n}, where each fold has size floor(n/K) or ceil(n/K). With foldwise cross-fitted nuisances etahat^{(-k)} and clip q in (0,1/2], define Vhat_{n,q}(pi)=n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}), where k(i) is the evaluation fold of observation i. Let j_n be the smallest index j such that Vhat_{n,q}(pi_j) >= sup_k Vhat_{n,q}(pi_k) - 1/n, and set pi_hat_n=pi_{j_n}. This measurable Pi-valued 1/n-ERM is the feasible clipped-AIPW ERM. The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter chosen by the bias-variance balance.

assumptions.ass:vc-localized-envelope: For the pointwise measurable finite-VC policy class Pi, every centered policy-indexed process with envelope B and conditional second moment bounded by C B^2 P_X(D_pi) has fixed-radius localized supremum bounded by C B n^{-1/2} r^{alpha/(2+2 alpha)}(log n)^p under thm:margin-localization.
assumptions.ass:fixed-crossfit-fold-count: The number K of cross-fitting folds in def:feasible-erm is a fixed finite integer independent of n, and the deterministic fold partition is balanced as stated there.

lem:crossfit-localized-offset-control.proof_tex:
Proof. Write the pooled centered cross-fit process as
\[
G_{cf}(\pi)=\sum_{k=1}^K w_k H_k(\pi),\qquad w_k=|I_k|/n,
\]
where \(H_k(\pi)\) is the centered evaluation-fold empirical average with the foldwise nuisance estimates held fixed. Conditional on the training folds, the increments in each \(H_k\) have envelope \(B\) and conditional second moment bounded by \(C B^2P_X(D_\pi)\), by the hypothesis of the lemma. The atomic offset assumption ass:vc-localized-offset-envelope is stated precisely for these finite-VC localized classes, including fixed-\(K\) cross-fitted evaluation-fold averages conditional on the training folds. Applying it foldwise gives
\[
E\left[\sup_{\pi\in\Pi}\{2|H_k(\pi)|-R_P(\pi)/4\}_+\mid \mathcal T_k\right]
\le C(B^2/n_k)^{A_\alpha}(\log n_k)^p,
\]
where \(n_k=|I_k|\). The regret localization required by that assumption is supplied by thm:margin-localization, whose bounded-outcome prerequisite is part of the displayed dependency list.

For the pooled process, use \(|\sum_k w_kH_k(\pi)|\le\sum_kw_k|H_k(\pi)|\) and convexity of \(x\mapsto x_+\):
\[
\{2|G_{cf}(\pi)|-R_P(\pi)/4\}_+
\le \sum_{k=1}^K w_k\{2|H_k(\pi)|-R_P(\pi)/4\}_+.
\]
Taking the supremum over \(\pi\), expectation, and then summing the foldwise bounds yields
\[
E_P\sup_{\pi\in\Pi}\{2|G_{cf}(\pi)|-R_P(\pi)/4\}_+
\le C\sum_{k=1}^K w_k(B^2/n_k)^{A_\alpha}(\log n_k)^p.
\]
By ass:fixed-crossfit-fold-count, \(K\) is fixed and the deterministic folds are balanced, so \(n_k\asymp n\) and \(\log n_k\le C\log n\). Hence the last display is at most
\[
C(B^2/n)^{A_\alpha}(\log n)^p.
\]
This proves the claimed offset control. No propensity-stability, oracle-denominator, or marginal-overlap condition is used.

snapshot.stmt: For the pooled cross-fit centered process G_cf(pi) from lem:crude-localized-master-bound, if the foldwise centered increments have envelope B and conditional second moment bounded by C B^2 P_X(D_pi), then under ass:fixed-crossfit-fold-count, ass:vc-localized-offset-envelope, and thm:margin-localization, E_P sup_{pi in Pi}{2|G_cf(pi)|-R_P(pi)/4}_+ <= C (B^2/n)^{A_alpha}(log n)^p.

With foldwise cross-fitted nuisances etahat^{(-k)} and clip q in (0,1/2], define Vhat_{n,q}(pi)=n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}), where k(i) is the evaluation fold of observation i. Let j_n be the smallest index j such that Vhat_{n,q}(pi_j) >= sup_k Vhat_{n,q}(pi_k) - 1/n, and set pi_hat_n=pi_{j_n}. This measurable Pi-valued 1/n-ERM is the feasible clipped-AIPW ERM. The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter chosen by the bias-variance balance.
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

assumptions.ass:fixed-crossfit-fold-count: The number K of cross-fitting folds in def:feasible-erm is a fixed finite integer independent of n, and the deterministic fold partition is balanced as stated there.
assumptions.ass:vc-localized-offset-envelope: For the same finite-VC localized classes, the offset positive part satisfies E sup_{pi in Pi}{2|z_pi|-R_P(pi)/4}_+ <= C (B^2/n)^{A_alpha}(log n)^p for centered processes with envelope B and conditional second moment bounded by C B^2 P_X(D_pi), including fixed-K cross-fitted evaluation-fold averages conditional on training folds.

lem:localized-vc-self-bound.proof_tex:
Proof. Set
\[
S=\sup_{\pi\in\Pi}\{2|z_\pi|-R_P(\pi)/4\}_+ .
\]
For the selected policy \(\widetilde\pi\), the assumed inequality gives
\[
R_P(\widetilde\pi)\le 2|z_{\widetilde\pi}|+\delta_n .
\]
Subtracting \(R_P(\widetilde\pi)/4\) from both sides gives
\[
\frac34R_P(\widetilde\pi)
\le 2|z_{\widetilde\pi}|-\frac14R_P(\widetilde\pi)+\delta_n
\le S+\delta_n .
\]
Therefore
\[
R_P(\widetilde\pi)\le \frac43S+\frac43\delta_n .
\]
Taking expectations and using the proved offset-control node lem:crossfit-localized-offset-control gives
\[
E_PR_P(\widetilde\pi)
\le C(B^2/n)^{A_\alpha}(\log n)^p+C\delta_n
=C\{\rho_n+\delta_n\}.
\]
Finally, \(A_\alpha=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)<1\) for finite \(\alpha\ge0\) and \(B\ge1\), so for all large \(n\), \(n^{-1}\le C(B^2/n)^{A_\alpha}(\log n)^p\) after increasing the constant. Thus any \(\delta_n\le1/n\) is absorbed into the \(C\rho_n\) term.

snapshot.stmt: Let rho_n=(B^2/n)^{A_alpha}(log n)^p with B>=1. For a centered Pi-indexed process z_pi whose offset positive part is controlled by the proved DAG node lem:crossfit-localized-offset-control, any Pi-indexed estimator pi_tilde satisfying R_P(pi_tilde) <= 2|z_{pi_tilde}| + delta_n obeys E_P R_P(pi_tilde) <= C{rho_n+delta_n}; if delta_n<=1/n, the delta_n term is absorbed into rho_n.

defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

lem:crude-localized-master-bound.proof_tex:
Proof. Fix \(P\) in def:law-class and fix the cross-fitted nuisance estimates satisfying the estimator-side conditions in the statement. Let \(\widehat\pi=\widehat\pi_n\). The proof is conditional on the training folds; all bounds are uniform in the folds and are then integrated over the training randomness.

For \(i\in I_k\), write
\[
\Gamma_i=\Gamma_q(O_i;\widehat\eta^{(-k)}),\qquad
m_k(x)=E_P[\Gamma_q(O;\widehat\eta^{(-k)})\mid X=x]=\tau_P(x)+b_k(x).
\]
The identity defining \(b_k\) is exactly lem:clip-bias applied with the foldwise nuisance triple. By lem:feasible-erm-basic-inequality, with comparator \(\pi_\star\in\Pi\) from ass:optimal-in-class,
\[
P_n[(\widehat\pi-\pi_\star)\Gamma_i]\ge -1/n .
\]
Define the pooled centered process
\[
G_{cf}(\pi)=P_n[(\pi-\pi_\star)\Gamma_i]-\sum_{k=1}^Kw_kP[(\pi-\pi_\star)m_k],\qquad w_k=|I_k|/n.
\]
Using def:welfare-regret and thm:welfare-identity,
\[
P[(\pi-\pi_\star)\tau_P]=-R_P(\pi),
\]
so the basic inequality gives, with \(r=R_P(\widehat\pi)\),
\[
r\le |G_{cf}(\widehat\pi)|+\left|\sum_{k=1}^Kw_kP[(\widehat\pi-\pi_\star)b_k]\right|+1/n .
\]
The stochastic process is controlled only through the crude clipped-score envelope. Lem:crude-clipped-score-envelope gives \(|\Gamma_i|\le C/q\), and since \(\pi-\pi_\star\) vanishes outside \(D_\pi\), the centered increments have envelope \(B=C/q\) and conditional second moment bounded by \(CB^2P_X(D_\pi)\). Hence lem:crossfit-localized-offset-control applies to \(G_{cf}\), and lem:localized-vc-self-bound will control the random-radius choice \(\widehat\pi\) once the deterministic drift is absorbed.

Assume \(\gamma>0\) and \(q\le c_ou^\gamma\). Lem:localized-clipped-drift-bound, applied foldwise and averaged over \(k\), gives
\[
\left|\sum_{k=1}^Kw_kP[(\widehat\pi-\pi_\star)b_k]\right|
\le C\left\{\frac{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}}q+r_{\mu,n}u^{\alpha/2}q^{1/(2\gamma)}+r_{\mu,n}(r/u)^{1/2}\right\}.
\]
By Young's inequality,
\[
Cr_{\mu,n}(r/u)^{1/2}\le r/4+Cr_{\mu,n}^2/u .
\]
Absorbing \(r/4\) into the left side yields
\[
r\le 2|G_{cf}(\widehat\pi)|+C\left\{\frac{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}}q+r_{\mu,n}u^{\alpha/2}q^{1/(2\gamma)}+\frac{r_{\mu,n}^2}{u}+\frac1n\right\}.
\]
Now apply lem:localized-vc-self-bound with \(B=C/q\). Since \(q\le1/2\) and \(A_\alpha\le1\), the \(1/n\) term is absorbed by the stochastic term for all large \(n\). This gives
\[
E_PR_P(\widehat\pi_n)
\le C\left\{(nq^2)^{-A_\alpha}+\frac{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}}q+r_{\mu,n}u^{\alpha/2}q^{1/(2\gamma)}+\frac{r_{\mu,n}^2}{u}\right\}(\log n)^p .
\]
The bounded oracle policy-learning centered process with score \((\pi-\pi_\star)\tau_P\) has envelope bounded by a universal constant and second moment bounded by \(CP_X(D_\pi)\); the same localized VC argument gives an additional valid oracle/localization contribution \(n^{-A_\alpha}(\log n)^p\). Because \(\beta_{\alpha,γ}\ge0\), def:exponents gives \(r_\star=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha+\beta_{\alpha,γ})\le A_\alpha\), so \(n^{-A_\alpha}\le n^{-r_\star}\). Retaining this benchmark term explicitly gives
\[
E_PR_P(\widehat\pi_n)
\le C\left\{n^{-r_\star}+(nq^2)^{-A_\alpha}+\frac{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}}q+r_{\mu,n}u^{\alpha/2}q^{1/(2\gamma)}+\frac{r_{\mu,n}^2}{u}\right\}(\log n)^p .
\]

For \(\gamma=0\), ass:strict-overlap-endpoint and fixed \(q\le\underline p/2\) imply, by lem:localized-clipped-drift-bound,
\[
\left|\sum_{k=1}^Kw_kP[(\widehat\pi-\pi_\star)b_k]\right|\le Cr_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}.
\]
The clipped-score envelope is then constant, so the same offset/self-bound argument gives
\[
E_PR_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le C\{n^{-A_\alpha}+r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}\}(\log n)^p .
\]
The proof uses the single data-driven ERM in def:feasible-erm and the crude \(q^{-2}\) second-moment scale; it does not use a marginal-tail shortcut, known propensity, oracle denominator, new law class, or clipped-propensity variance-stability premise.

snapshot.stmt: Under def:law-class and the estimator-side/policy conditions ass:optimal-in-class, ass:nuisance-rate, ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances, ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents, ass:policy-class, and ass:vc-localized-envelope, the cross-fitted clipped-AIPW 1/n-ERM of def:feasible-erm satisfies E_P R_P(pi_hat_n) <= C{n^{-r_star}+(n q^2)^{-A_alpha}+r_mu_n r_e_n/q+r_mu_n u^{alpha/2}q^{1/(2 gamma)}+r_mu_n^2/u}(log n)^p for gamma>0 whenever q<=c_o u^gamma. For gamma=0 with fixed q<=underline_p/2 it satisfies E_P R_P(pi_hat_n)<=C{n^{-A_alpha}+r_mu_n r_e_n}(log n)^p.

defs.def:law-class: { P : ass:iid, ass:consistency, ass:unconfoundedness, ass:bounded-outcome, ass:positivity, ass:margin, ass:zero-effect, ass:overlap-decay, and ass:strict-overlap-endpoint hold at exponents (alpha,gamma) }
defs.def:feasible-erm: Fix a countable pointwise-dense subclass Pi_0=(pi_j)_{j>=1} from ass:policy-class and a deterministic balanced K-fold partition I_1,...,I_K of {1,...,n}, where each fold has size floor(n/K) or ceil(n/K). With foldwise cross-fitted nuisances etahat^{(-k)} and clip q in (0,1/2], define Vhat_{n,q}(pi)=n^{-1} sum_i pi(X_i) Gamma_q(O_i; etahat^{(-k(i))}), where k(i) is the evaluation fold of observation i. Let j_n be the smallest index j such that Vhat_{n,q}(pi_j) >= sup_k Vhat_{n,q}(pi_k) - 1/n, and set pi_hat_n=pi_{j_n}. This measurable Pi-valued 1/n-ERM is the feasible clipped-AIPW ERM. The clip q is the analysis tuning parameter chosen by the bias-variance balance.
defs.def:clipped-aipw-score: Gamma_q(O; eta) = mu_1(X) - mu_0(X) + (A/e_q(X;eta))(Y - mu_1(X)) - ((1-A)/(1-e_q(X;eta)))(Y - mu_0(X)).
defs.def:welfare-regret: V_P(pi) = E_P[pi(X) tau_P(X)]; pi_star(x) = 1{tau_P(x) >= 0}; R_P(pi) = V_P(pi_star) - V_P(pi).
defs.def:exponents: beta_ag = (0 if gamma = 0 else alpha gamma/(alpha+1)); D_ag = 2 + alpha + beta_ag; r_star(alpha,gamma) = (1+alpha)/D_ag.

assumptions.ass:optimal-in-class: pi_star is an element of Pi for every law P in the class.
assumptions.ass:nuisance-rate: The cross-fitted nuisance estimators satisfy ||muhat_a-mu_a||_{L2(P)} <= r_mu_n and ||ehat-e||_{L2(P)} <= r_e_n for a in {0,1}, with product-rate r_mu_n r_e_n = O(n^{-1/2}).
assumptions.ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances: The cross-fitted outcome-regression estimates muhat_0, muhat_1 take values in [-1,1].
assumptions.ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents: There exist a >= 0, c >= 1/2 and constants C_mu, C_prod such that r_mu_n <= C_mu n^(-a) and r_mu_n r_e_n <= C_prod n^(-c) for all large n.
assumptions.ass:policy-class: Pi is a pointwise measurable class of measurable deterministic policies with finite VC dimension d_Pi < infinity: there is a countable subclass Pi_0 subset Pi such that every pi in Pi is the pointwise limit of a sequence from Pi_0.
assumptions.ass:vc-localized-envelope: For the pointwise measurable finite-VC policy class Pi, every centered policy-indexed process with envelope B and conditional second moment bounded by C B^2 P_X(D_pi) has fixed-radius localized supremum bounded by C B n^{-1/2} r^{alpha/(2+2 alpha)}(log n)^p under thm:margin-localization.
assumptions.ass:strict-overlap-endpoint: When gamma=0, p_P(X) >= underline_p holds P_X-almost surely, for a constant underline_p in (0,1/2].
assumptions.ass:fixed-crossfit-fold-count: The number K of cross-fitting folds in def:feasible-erm is a fixed finite integer independent of n, and the deterministic fold partition is balanced as stated there.

oeq:feasible-upper.proof_tex:
Proof. The causal target is the welfare regret \(R_P(\pi)=V_P(\pi_\star)-V_P(\pi)\), whose identifying functional is fixed in def:welfare-regret. This result concerns the estimation behavior of the explicit cross-fit clipped-AIPW \(1/n\)-ERM in def:feasible-erm.

Fix one nuisance regime \((a,c,C_\mu,C_{prod})\). The upper-risk functional in def:upper-risk takes the supremum over laws in def:law-class together with the explicitly stated estimator-side and policy-side conditions: ass:optimal-in-class, ass:nuisance-rate, ass:bounded-crossfit-nuisances, ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents, ass:policy-class, ass:vc-localized-envelope, ass:vc-localized-offset-envelope, and ass:fixed-crossfit-fold-count. These conditions are not asserted to follow from def:law-class.

First let \(\gamma>0\). Def:feasible-rate chooses \(q_n=q_0n^{-s_{feas}}\) and \(u_n=\bar u n^{-t_{feas}}\) on the compact feasible set \(0\le s_{feas}\le1/2\), \(0\le t_{feas}\le s_{feas}/\gamma\). Hence \(q_n\le c_ou_n^\gamma\) for all large \(n\), and lem:crude-localized-master-bound applies:
\[
E_PR_P(\widehat\pi_n)
\le C\left\{n^{-r_\star}+(nq_n^2)^{-A_\alpha}+\frac{r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}}{q_n}+r_{\mu,n}u_n^{\alpha/2}q_n^{1/(2\gamma)}+\frac{r_{\mu,n}^2}{u_n}\right\}(\log n)^p.
\]
By lem:clip-balance-exponent and ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents, the four feasible stochastic/nuisance terms are bounded by \(Cn^{-g_{joint}(\alpha,\gamma,a,c)}\). Therefore
\[
E_PR_P(\widehat\pi_n)
\le Cn^{-\min\{r_\star,g_{joint}\}}(\log n)^p
=Cn^{-r_{up}}(\log n)^p,
\]
where \(r_{up}=r_{feas}\) is the exponent constructed in def:feasible-rate.

For \(\gamma=0\), def:feasible-rate uses fixed \(q_n=q_0\le\underline p/2\). Lem:crude-localized-master-bound gives
\[
E_PR_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le C\{n^{-A_\alpha}+r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}\}(\log n)^p.
\]
Since ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents gives \(r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}\le Cn^{-c}\),
\[
E_PR_P(\widehat\pi_n)\le Cn^{-\min\{A_\alpha,c\}}(\log n)^p=Cn^{-r_{up}}(\log n)^p.
\]
Taking the supremum over the conditional domain in def:upper-risk proves
\[
U_n(\alpha,\gamma,a,c;\widehat\eta)\le Cn^{-r_{up}}(\log n)^p.
\]
This derives the closed honest exponent for the plain feasible clipped-AIPW ERM from the crude \(q^{-2}\) score envelope and the deterministic drift controls; no new law-class split or rate-buying propensity stability assumption is introduced.

snapshot.stmt: CONDITIONAL ACHIEVABILITY / CONSTRUCT-AND-DETERMINE. This is a conditional achievability bound over def:law-class plus explicit side conditions, not a smaller law class and not a claim that nuisance rates or optimal-in-class are implied by def:law-class.

lem:clip-balance-exponent.proof_tex:
Proof. Let \(A_\alpha=(1+\alpha)/(2+\alpha)\). Suppose first that \(\gamma>0\). For a feasible schedule \(q_n=q_0n^{-s}\), \(u_n=\bar u n^{-t}\) with \(0\le s\le1/2\) and \(0\le t\le s/\gamma\), the normalization in def:feasible-rate gives \(q_n\le c_ou_n^\gamma\) for all large \(n\). The four non-oracle terms in lem:crude-localized-master-bound have exponents
\[
(nq_n^2)^{-A_\alpha}=C n^{-A_\alpha(1-2s)},
\]
\[
r_{\mu,n}r_{e,n}/q_n\le Cn^{-(c-s)},
\]
\[
r_{\mu,n}u_n^{\alpha/2}q_n^{1/(2\gamma)}\le Cn^{-\{a+s/(2\gamma)+\alpha t/2\}},
\]
and
\[
r_{\mu,n}^2/u_n\le Cn^{-(2a-t)}.
\]
The last three displays use ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents. Thus, for this \((s,t)\), the feasible part of the master bound is at most \(Cn^{-\phi(s,t)}\), where
\[
\phi(s,t)=\min\{A_\alpha(1-2s),c-s,a+s/(2\gamma)+\alpha t/2,2a-t\}.
\]
The function \(\phi\) is continuous on the compact feasible set, so the maximizer chosen in def:feasible-rate exists and has value \(g_{joint}\). Consequently the gamma-positive upper exponent is
\[
r_{up}=r_{feas}=\min\{r_\star(\alpha,\gamma),g_{joint}(\alpha,\gamma,a,c)\}.
\]
If \(\gamma=0\), the clip is fixed, so the stochastic term has exponent \(A_\alpha\), and ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents gives the product-nuisance exponent \(c\). Therefore \(r_{up}=r_{feas}=\min\{A_\alpha,c\}\).

snapshot.stmt: For the deterministic schedule in def:feasible-rate, the terms in lem:crude-localized-master-bound optimize to the exponent r_up=r_feas. For gamma>0, with A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha), q_n=q_0 n^{-s_feas}, u_n=u_bar n^{-t_feas}, and g_joint=max_{0<=s<=1/2, 0<=t<=s/gamma} min{A_alpha(1-2s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)+alpha t/2, 2a-t}, the master bound is at most C{n^{-r_star}+n^{-g_joint}}(log n)^p = C n^{-min{r_star,g_joint}}(log n)^p. For gamma=0, the exponent is min{A_alpha,c}.

Let (s_feas,t_feas) be any maximizer, set g_joint=phi(s_feas,t_feas), q_n=q_0 n^{-s_feas}, and u_n=u_bar n^{-t_feas}. r_{up}=r_{feas}=\min\{r_\star(\alpha,\gamma),g_{joint}(\alpha,\gamma,a,c)\}.
\]
If \(\gamma=0\), the clip is fixed, so the stochastic term has exponent \(A_\alpha\), and ass:polynomial-nuisance-exponents gives the product-nuisance exponent \(c\). Therefore \(r_{up}=r_{feas}=\min\{A_\alpha,c\}\).

oeq:feasible-tight.proof_tex: If g_joint(alpha,gamma,a,c)>=r_star(alpha,gamma), then oeq:feasible-upper and thm:minimax-lower match up to logarithmic factors for the explicit feasible ERM: c n^{-r_star} <= M_n and U_n <= C n^{-r_star}(log n)^p. If g_joint<r_star, the current results give the honest bracket c n^{-r_star} for the baseline minimax lower benchmark and U_n <= C n^{-g_joint}(log n)^p for the specified feasible clipped-AIPW ERM, with tightness unresolved.

snapshot.stmt: OPEN: In the strict-gap branch of the conditional feasible achievability bound for the regime-indexed risk U_n over def:law-class plus explicit side conditions, determine whether any genuinely feasible estimator under estimated cross-fitted nuisances can attain the converse exponent r_star(alpha,gamma), or whether weak-arm nuisance learning imposes the slower conditional exponent derived in oeq:feasible-upper.

defs.def:feasible-rate: For one fixed nuisance regime (a,c,C_mu,C_prod), let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha). For gamma>0, fix u_bar in (0,u_0] and q_0 in (0,min{1/2,c_o u_bar^gamma}]. Define phi(s,t)=min{A_alpha(1-2s), c-s, a+s/(2 gamma)+alpha t/2, 2a-t} on the compact feasible set 0<=s<=1/2 and 0<=t<=s/gamma. Let (s_feas,t_feas) be any maximizer, set g_joint=phi(s_feas,t_feas), q_n=q_0 n^{-s_feas}, and u_n=u_bar n^{-t_feas}. Then q_n<=c_o u_n^gamma for all large n, and the solved conditional feasible upper exponent is r_up=r_feas=min{r_star(alpha,gamma),g_joint(alpha,gamma,a,c)}. For gamma=0, use fixed q_n=q_0<=underline_p/2 and set r_up=r_feas=min{A_alpha,c}.

statements.current: Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha) and kappa=alpha/(2+2 alpha). Suppose a Pi-indexed estimator pi_tilde obeys a self-localized basic inequality R_P(pi_tilde) <= 2 Z_n(R_P(pi_tilde)) + delta_n, where Z_n(r) is a nondecreasing process satisfying E_P Z_n(r) <= C B n^(-1/2) r^kappa (log n)^p for every r. Then E_P R_P(pi_tilde) <= C{(B^2/n)^{A_alpha}(log n)^p + delta_n}. If delta_n<=1/n, the delta_n term is absorbed into the same rate.
statements.proposed: Let A_alpha=(1+alpha)/(2+alpha) and kappa=alpha/(2+2 alpha). Let z_pi be a centered Pi-indexed process and Z_n(r)=sup_{pi in Pi:R_P(pi)<=r}|z_pi|. Suppose a Pi-indexed estimator pi_tilde obeys R_P(pi_tilde) <= 2 Z_n(R_P(pi_tilde)) + delta_n. Suppose also that, for rho_n=(B^2/n)^{A_alpha}(log n)^p with B>=1, the localized offset-peeling condition E_P sup_{pi in Pi}{2|z_pi|-R_P(pi)/4}_+ <= C rho_n holds. Then E_P R_P(pi_tilde) <= C{rho_n+delta_n}. If delta_n<=1/n, the delta_n term is absorbed into the same rate.
statements.reason: The current fixed-radius expectation hypothesis alone is too weak for a random-radius self-bound: an arbitrary monotone process can have small E Z_n(r) at each deterministic r but rare large paths that force E R_P(pi_tilde) of order B n^{-1/2}, not the fast fixed-point rate. The localized VC machinery supplies the standard offset-peeling positive-part control, and the proof uses exactly that stronger process condition.

definitions.reason: The proof derives the same crude q^{-2} balance but makes the optimizer a compact finite-dimensional maximization. Since phi(s,t)<=0 for s>1/2 while phi(0,0)>=0, the original supremum over s>=0 is attained on 0<=s<=1/2; the corrected construction exposes this computable maximizer and the proof's u_n schedule explicitly.

assumptions.condition: The number K of cross-fitting folds in def:feasible-erm is a fixed finite integer independent of n, and the deterministic fold partition is balanced as stated there.
assumptions.reason: The pooled cross-fit empirical-process reduction uses sum_k (n_k/n)n_k^{-1/2}=O(n^{-1/2}); if K grew with n this factor would be O((K/n)^{1/2}).
assumptions.standard_or_novel: standard: fixed-K cross-fitting, as in Chernozhukov et al. (2018) DML
assumptions.not_crux: This is an estimator-side bookkeeping condition for the fold reduction; it does not assert any regret rate, drift cancellation, overlap stability, or nuisance learning improvement.
